query: recent studies on valproic acid effectiveness in improving survival for diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma. Review the appropriate process for the evaluation of diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma. Outline the management options available for diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the care of diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation oncologists are consulted immediately after diagnosis to plan further treatment. Even though there have been tremendous advancements in radiation and research in oncology, outcomes for survival for children with DIPGs have not changed significantly over the past 20 years. [27] Conventionally fractionated radiotherapy is still the standard of care for children with DIPGs, but hypofractionated radiotherapy has shown promising results when compared with conventional radiotherapy. [27] Radiotherapy is usually given to the tumor for a total dose of 54 Gy for six weeks (1.8-Gy daily fractions). Hypofractionated radiation with 39 Gy using fewer fractions have similar outcomes and is better tolerated in young children. [28] [29] Re-irradiation for tumor progression can be considered with 25 Gy given over ten fractions producing a mild symptom or survival benefit. [30] [31] Radiosensitizing agents do not improve survival.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (DIPG) is an aggressive tumor of the brainstem. It almost always affects the pediatric population, with approximately 200 to 300 new annual cases in the United States. Most patients survive less than one year. [1] These patients were primarily treated with radiation therapy, as surgical resection was not a viable option for them. The effects of radiation therapy are temporary, and no chemotherapeutic agent has been effective in treating this pathology. Several clinical trials of new agents and novel therapeutic approaches have emerged over the past decades to improve the outcome of patients with DIPGs, yet no success has been obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (DIPG) is an aggressive type of childhood cancer that forms in the brainstem. They are very rare and almost always occur in the pediatric population. To avoid the high morbidity and mortality associated with this condition, DPIG must be promptly diagnosed and treated. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of diffuse intrinsic pontine gliomas and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: While the pediatrician is almost always involved in the care of a patient with DIPG, it is essential to consult with an interprofessional team of specialists that include a neurosurgeon, neuro-oncologist, and radiation oncologist. The nurses are also vital members of the interprofessional group as they will monitor the patient's vital signs and assist with the education of the patient and the family. In the perioperative period for pain, wound infection, and nausea/vomit, the pharmacist will ensure that the patient is on the right analgesics, antiemetics, and appropriate antibiotics. The problem gets even more complicated when the parents suffer the emotional burden of the dismal prognosis of DIPG. Early phase interaction with psychologists, psychiatrists, and family therapists are needed to handle this critical issue.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with DIPG and family members should have a complete understanding of the disease and prognosis to have realistic expectations. The timeline of how early the patient is diagnosed will have an important part in how the family reacts to the situation. It is why, in the early phase, psychologists, psychiatrists, and family therapists should form part of the team. The realities of deficits, physical impediments, and overall conditions after treatments should be clearly explained to the parents. Thorough training of how to manage these situations is necessary, including nasogastric nutrition, foley catheter, the possibility of being bedridden, and sacral ulcers.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Several clinical trials of various systemic therapies, including stem cell transplantation, temozolomide, gemcitabine, capecitabine, and tyrosine kinase inhibitors have been tested or are ongoing. Still, none have proved to increase the survival of children with DIPGs. [15] [32] Convection-enhanced drug delivery systems have brought hope to the treatments of DIPGs. Improvements in this field may provide better drug delivery systems and target these tumor cells directly, especially at the normal brain tissue interface. [33] A new therapy for the management of DIPG is adjuvant multimodal immunotherapy. This therapy consists of vaccination cycles (Newcastle disease virus, mature dendritic cells) and immunogenic cell death therapy (temozolomide, local modulated electro-hyperthermia), offering improved overall survival but warrants further investigation. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Neuro oncologist Radiation oncologist Neuroradiologist Neurosurgeon Neurologist Neuropathologist Pediatric intensivist

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Steroids, specifically dexamethasone, are given, and the goal of treatment is the stabilization of neurological symptoms until possible neurosurgical biopsy or radiation therapy. Complications due to long term use should be avoided; thus, it should be weaned off as quickly as tolerated. [19] [20] Steroids stabilize the blood-brain barrier and can affect tissue penetration of systemic therapies. [21] Additionally, dexamethasone use in high-grade gliomas is associated with worse overall survival. [22] During the management, less than 10% of patients diagnosed with DIPGs develop hydrocephalus and require a third ventriculostomy or ventricular shunt. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of DPIG is dismal due to the absence of effective therapies. This tumor is the principal cause of death among brain tumors affecting childhood. Most patients are diagnosed before seven years of age. After diagnosis, median survival is usually nine months. Only 10% live for more than two years. [35] When compared to pediatric glioblastoma, the prognosis for DIPG is the worst with less overall survival.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: An early consideration should be the consultation of advanced pediatric care or pediatric palliative care specialists. Offering palliative care services to all patients with DIPG early in the course of the disease provides support for the immediate and ongoing physical, psychological, and social effects this lethal disease will have on the patient and the family. Combined neuro-oncologic and palliative care support maximize how patients and families perform during their treatment and can even help to identify disease-related changes before clinically or radiographically suspected progression. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The most important step in management is obtaining a tissue diagnosis. First, it characterizes the lesion for classification and treatment, and second, it is used for research and development of more effective management strategies. Nevertheless, there has been a historical resistance to biopsy of these tumors, because of anatomical access. Fortunately, the technology has advanced, and stereotactic biopsy is a safe and minimally invasive procedure that has a very low complication rate. Based on the literature, a stereotactic biopsy of DIPG is as safe and diagnostic as a supratentorial biopsy, and the amount of tissue in the biopsy allows for analysis, including whole-genome sequencing. [17] [24] [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Procarbazine, lomustine, and vincristine, typically delivered adjuvant after radiotherapy or surgery, is one of the original treatment regimens investigated prospectively. RTOG 9802 investigated the use of adjuvant PCV after radiotherapy in patients with high-risk low-grade gliomas. [47] While the initial reports did not show a survival benefit, the reports on longer-term follow-up demonstrated a significant increase in 10-year progression-free survival (21% vs. 51%) and overall survival (60% vs. 40%). On sub-analysis, the benefit appeared to be confined to IDH-mutated gliomas. The PCV regimen continues to be evaluated in prospective trials in more aggressive tumor types, such as in the CODEL trial (NCT00887146). The regimen is typically delivered over six cycles Procarbazine at a dose of 60mg/m2 orally per day on days 8-21 of each cycle, Lomustine at a dose of 110mg/m2 orally on day 1 of each cycle, and vincristine at a dose of 1.4mg/m2 (maximum dose of 2mg) administered intravenously on days 8 and 29 of each cycle. [53] Toxicities associated with this regimen include bone marrow suppression resulting in thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, and anemia, with 45% experiencing severe suppression. Hepatotoxicity, neurotoxicity, allergic drug reaction, nausea, and vomiting have also been documented in significant percentages of patients undergoing this treatment regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Brainstem gliomas affect approximately 300 children in the United States each year and are the major cause of death in children with brain tumors. The most common of these is DIPG comprising 80% of the cases. Incidence is 1 to 2 cases per 100,000 population. DIPG has a peak incidence of 6-9 years and a slight preference for males. [6] Among all the tumors in children, they represent 20% of them.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: Within the valproate series, Valproat functions as a parent entity with child compounds such as valproic acid and its salts, while lamotrigine and tiagabine are identified as child agents within the broader antiepileptic hierarchy. These medications collectively provide a comprehensive pharmacologic approach to controlling seizures and related neurological manifestations. Clinical observations note a frequent comorbid association between epilepsy and headache (صداع), emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management. Developmental disorders in children are closely linked to broader nervous system disorders, and withdrawal‑type seizures represent a specific manifestation of these conditions. Nervous system disorders may arise secondary to malignant brain tumors, which are cancers of the brain and nervous system typically located in the large brain (velký mozek) and often accompanied by increased intracranial pressure (تكثر الدبق الدماغي). Increased intracranial pressure and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1054
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة (generalized‑onset non‑movement seizure). This seizure type is classified under epilepsy; convulsions, indicating that epilepsy with convulsions is considered a manifestation of نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة. Both cancer of brain and nervous system and epilepsy; convulsions have their primary finding site in the velký mozek (the cerebrum), highlighting the central role of the brain in these disorders. Cancer of brain and nervous system is a specific form of ورم الدماغ الخبيث (malignant brain tumor), placing it within the broader category of malignant brain neoplasms. Together, these relationships underscore the anatomical focus on the cerebrum for both neoplastic and epileptic conditions and identify mefobarbital as a therapeutic option for the associated generalized seizure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2493
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat generalized onset seizures, offering therapeutic benefit in controlling these seizure episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the brain and nervous system is a malignant brain tumor (ورم الدماغ الخبيث) that typically originates in the cerebrum (velký mozek) and is associated with the descriptor تكثر الدبق الدماغي. Epilepsy with convulsions also localizes to the cerebrum and is classified as a generalized onset seizure characterized by loss of movement (نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة). The antiepileptic drug mefobarbital may be used to treat this type of generalized seizure. Recognizing that both the brain tumor and the seizure disorder involve the same cerebral region aids clinicians in coordinated diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 234
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, these relationships integrate neurological disorders (hydrocephalus, dementia, seizures, dizziness), their neurosurgical interventions (ventricular shunts), underlying etiologies (malignant brain tumor, abnormal CSF), pharmacologic treatment (meclizine), and a comprehensive imaging framework anchored by routine chest radiography that supports diagnostic evaluation across the brain, chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 935
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of brain and nervous system, a malignant brain tumor (ورم الدماغ الخبيث), commonly involves the cerebrum (velký mozek) as its site of pathology and is linked to the manifestation of increased intracranial pressure (تكثر الدبق الدماغي). Epilepsy with convulsions also localizes to the cerebrum (velký mozek) and is classified as a generalized‑onset seizure (نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة), a condition that may be treated with mefobarbital. Increased intracranial pressure can present as headache (صداع), and lumbar puncture (البزل القطني (LP)) is clinically associated with post‑procedure headache. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of cerebral disease sites, seizure classification, therapeutic options, and procedural complications in comprehensive patient care.

The same brain lesion site implicated in epilepsy is also involved in brain and nervous‑system cancers, reinforcing the link between seizure disorders, intracranial fluid accumulation, and headache. Recognition of the extensive antiepileptic drug repertoire, their enzyme‑mediated interactions, shared P‑glycoprotein inhibition, and the common comorbidities of headache, CSF abnormalities, and malignant brain lesions guides comprehensive, individualized patient care.

It is associated with vascular mechanisms (صداع وعائي) and can present as familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية), ocular migraine (الشقيقة الشالة للعين) and as part of cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant type 1 with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy, which also produces gait disturbance (اضطراب المشية). Preventive therapies include topiramate and valproic acid (kyselina valproová). Migraine is linked anatomically to the nervous system (nervový systém) and is related to other headache entities such as vascular headache and general headache (صداع). Disorders of the nervous system (اضطراب الجهاز العصبي) encompass developmental disorders in children, head injury (إصابة في الرأس), withdrawal seizures (اختلاجات انسحابية), abnormal and unspecified cerebrospinal fluid findings (اكتشافات غير طبيعية وغير محددة في السائل الدماغي النخاعي), malignant brain tumors

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1031
retrieved_docs.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) is the anatomical site associated with various other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions. Interventions for these conditions may involve the procedure site of muscular neuro blockade (إحصار عصبي عضلي), indicating that targeted blockade techniques are relevant to nervous system pathology. The condition described as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور) is linked to these hereditary and degenerative disorders, and valproate (Valproat) is specifically contraindicated in patients with this diffuse encephalitis. Recognizing the nervous system’s role as the primary anatomic site for hereditary and degenerative disorders, the application of muscular neuro blockade, and the contraindication of valproate in diffuse encephalitis guides clinical decision‑making and optimizes patient care.

Neurological disorder encompasses developmental disorders in children (vývojové poruchy u dětí) and specific forms such as head injury (إصابة في الرأس) and withdrawal seizures (اختلاجات انسحابية). It may arise secondary to malignant brain tumor (ورم الدماغ الخبيث) or to unspecified abnormal findings in cerebrospinal fluid (اكتشافات غير طبيعية وغير محددة في السائل الدماغي النخاعي), both identified through screening for suspected conditions. Malignant brain tumor is the parent condition for brain and nervous system cancer, which is associated with increased brain fluid (تكثر الدبق الدماغي) and has its primary anatomic finding in the large brain (velký mozek). This integrated understanding supports comprehensive assessment and targeted management of patients presenting with neurological symptoms.

Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy type 1, illustrates the overlap between vascular cerebral disease, gait disorder, and migraine, emphasizing the interconnected nature of vascular, headache, and neurodegenerative processes. Overall, the community’s knowledge highlights the anatomical sites (brain, ventricles, nervous system), clinical associations (dementia, ischemic complications, cranial nerve dysfunction, gait disturbance), genetic and familial forms, and the role of neurosurgical procedures (extracranial ventricular shunting, nasopharyngeal diversion) and pharmacologic prevention (sodium valproate) in integrated diagnosis and management of these interrelated neurological conditions.
